Bagged in the first scenario the author is dealt two cards from a standard deck of 52 cards. The goal is to calculate the probability of getting at least one card of a specific suit, such as hearts, in the next 5 cards dealt. The total number of ways to choose 5 cards from the remaining 50 cards is given by the combination function C(50, 5). The number of ways to choose 5 cards with no hearts is C(39, 5) since there are 39 cards not of the heart suit. The probability of not getting any hearts is C(39, 5) divided by C(50, 5). Therefore the probability of getting at least one heart is 1 minus C(39, 5) divided by C(50, 5). This closed form expression captures the likelihood of the author having a favorable hand based on the given conditions.

Bagged in the second scenario the author is faced with a decision between two strategies. To evaluate the expected value of each strategy the author must multiply the probability of each outcome by the corresponding payoff and sum the results. For Strategy A the expected value is 0.4 times 1000 plus 0.6 times negative 500. This simplifies to 400 minus 300 which equals 100. For Strategy B the expected value is 0.6 times 800 plus 0.4 times negative 200. This simplifies to 480 minus 80 which equals 400. Based on the highest expected value the author should choose Strategy B. The expected value of Strategy B is greater than that of Strategy A.
